content.assessment: Investing in comprehensive-school quality directly targets several psychology-relevant mechanisms: addressing teacher shortages and instructional quality could improve the conditions for competence-support and autonomy, both central to self-determination theory, and could reduce demand-driven stratification if families' selective-track preference is partly a quality-avoidance response. This proposal does not change selection timing or structure directly, so existing reference-group and big-fish-little-pond dynamics for currently selected/non-selected students would persist unless quality improvements are large enough to shift enrollment patterns. The proposal's own risk analysis notes that track demand may be driven by status-signaling rather than quality perception alone, which limits confidence that psychological benefits (e.g., reduced anxiety about
opportunities: Improved school climate and instructional quality could support mastery-oriented goal structures in comprehensive schools, potentially countering performance-orientation pressures associated with the selective sector
strengths: Addressing teacher shortages and instructional quality directly targets competence-support conditions central to self-determination theory
If successful, could reduce family motivation to seek early selective placement, indirectly reducing the number of students exposed to early-track reference-group transitions
threats: Demographic decline and school closures could offset psychological gains from quality investment if disadvantaged students end up concentrated in fewer, more strained comprehensive schools despite improved average quality
weaknesses: Does not alter selection structure or timing, so existing big-fish-little-pond and labeling dynamics tied to current tracks remain unaddressed in the near term
Proposal's own analysis acknowledges track demand may be status-driven rather than purely quality-driven, undermining confidence that psychological benefits will follow from quality investment alone
